title: Why your body flinches at kindness after prolonged neglect
author: Kalesh

The kindness that lands like a threat 

 Someone holds the door for you. A coworker says they appreciate your work. A friend offers to bring you soup when...
htmlContent:
The kindness that lands like a threat

Someone holds the door for you. A coworker says they appreciate your work. A friend offers to bring you soup when you're sick. And instead of warmth, instead of gratitude, something inside you tightens. Your shoulders go up. Your jaw clenches. Your chest gets tight. You feel a surge of something that might be fear, might be suspicion, might be a quiet voice saying "what do they want?"

You're not ungrateful. You're not broken. You're not a bad person. Here's the thing. Your nervous system has learned something that your conscious mind may not have caught up with yet. It has learned that kindness is not safe. That care comes with a cost. That softness is a setup for something hard.

And your body, being the faithful guardian it is, flinches. It flinches because it remembers what your mind has tried to forget.

What the nervous system learns from neglect

When you grow up in an environment where your needs are consistently ignored, where your distress is met with coldness or punishment, where affection is conditional or absent, your nervous system doesn't just feel sad. It adapts. It rewires. It builds a survival strategy.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, would tell you that your body is constantly scanning for cues of safety and danger. This is called neuroception. It happens below conscious awareness, faster than thought. Your nervous system reads tone of voice, facial expression, posture, pace of approach. And it makes a split-second decision: safe, dangerous, or life-threatening.

When you've lived through prolonged neglect, your neuroception gets calibrated to a different baseline. Kindness doesn't register as safe. It registers as unfamiliar, and the brain treats unfamiliar as potentially dangerous. A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link), which walks through exactly how this wiring happens and how to gently shift it.

Your body learned that the people who were supposed to care for you were not reliable. That their attention could flip. That their warmth could turn cold without warning. So now, when someone is kind, your body doesn't relax into it. It braces. It waits for the other shoe to drop. Because in your experience, the other shoe always dropped.

Something happens in the body when safety has been absent for a long time. The muscles forget how to relax. Not metaphorically - literally. The fascia tightens. The diaphragm restricts. The psoas - that deep hip flexor that connects your spine to your legs - stays chronically contracted, ready to run or curl into a ball at any moment.

Bessel van der Kolk's research on yoga and trauma showed something worth noticing. Traumatized people who practiced yoga showed changes in their brain scans - specifically in the insula, the part of the brain responsible for interoception, for feeling what's happening inside your own body. Many trauma survivors have learned to disconnect from body sensation entirely. It was too dangerous to feel. Yoga - gentle, trauma-informed yoga - helped them come back.

I'm not saying yoga fixes everything. I'm saying the body needs to be included in healing. You can't think your way out of something that lives below thought. You have to feel your way through it. Slowly. With support. At your own pace. There's no rushing this.

This isn't a character flaw. It's a biological adaptation that kept you alive. Think about it. If you had relaxed into your neglectful caregiver's rare moments of attention, you would have been devastated when they withdrew again. The flinch, the bracing, the suspicion, it protected you from the crash. It kept you from hoping too hard. From wanting too much.

Why the flinch is so hard to unlearn

Here's where it gets tricky. The flinch isn't just a mental habit you can talk yourself out of. It's encoded in your body, in your nervous system, in the way your vagus nerve responds to social cues. It's not something you can think your way through.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, has spent decades showing that trauma lives in the body, not in the story. You can understand intellectually that someone is being genuinely kind. You can tell yourself "this person is safe, they're not like my parents, I can trust them." And your body can still say "nope, I don't buy it."

Your body has its own memory. It remembers the years of being ignored. The meals that weren't made. The tears that weren't wiped. The achievements that weren't celebrated. The pain that wasn't seen. And it remembers that the moments of kindness that did come were often followed by something worse. A hug that turned into a lecture. A gift that came with strings. A compliment that was used against you later.

So your body made a rule. Kindness equals danger. Care equals cost. Softness equals vulnerability, and vulnerability equals getting hurt. That rule kept you safe for a long time. But now it's getting in the way. Now it's keeping you from receiving what you actually need.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link), which explains how trauma gets trapped in the body and what you can do about it.

The paradox of needing what you fear

This is the cruelest part of the whole thing. You need kindness. You need care. You need attunement and warmth and softness. These are biological necessities, not luxuries. Your nervous system requires co-regulation to function well. Your body needs the soothing presence of another to down-regulate stress. Humans are not meant to self-regulate alone. We're pack animals. We need each other.

Your body remembers what your mind has forgotten. That's not poetry - that's neuroscience. Peter Levine spent decades documenting how trauma lives in the tissues, in the fascia, in the way your muscles hold tension patterns that mirror the exact posture you were in when the worst thing happened.

I've seen this in my own body. The way my shoulders round forward when I feel threatened - that's the fetal position, compressed into a standing posture. The way my jaw clenches before I even know I'm stressed - that's years of swallowed words, swallowed screams, swallowed truth. Your body is an archive. It stores everything.

Here's what I want you to try. Put one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Don't try to change anything. Just notice. Is your breath high and shallow? Is your belly tight? Are your shoulders up by your ears? Whatever you find, it's okay. You're not doing it wrong. You're just meeting yourself where you are. That's the beginning of everything.

But the very thing you need the most is the thing your body has learned to fear the most. So you push it away. You sabotage relationships. You pick fights. You withdraw. You keep people at arm's length. You tell yourself you don't need anyone. You become fiercely independent, and you're proud of it.

And underneath all that independence is a child who is still waiting for someone to notice they're hungry. Still waiting for someone to see they're hurting. Still waiting for the kindness that never came reliably.

Wild, right? Your survival strategy is also your prison. The thing that kept you alive is now keeping you from living.

Gabor Mate, a physician who has written extensively about the connection between childhood adversity and adult illness, talks about this constantly. He says that the question isn't "what's wrong with you?" but "what happened to you?" Your flinch response isn't pathology. It's adaptation. It's your body's best attempt to protect you in an environment where protection wasn't available.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link), which explores how suppressed emotion and chronic vigilance wear down the body over time.

How to start softening the flinch

You can't force your nervous system to trust. You can't bully it into feeling safe. But you can work with it. You can create conditions where it might, over time, begin to relax its guard.

Start small. Really small. Like, absurdly small. Don't try to accept a compliment gracefully. Just notice that you flinched. Don't try to let someone hug you. Just notice that your shoulders went up. Don't try to trust. Just notice what happens in your body when you consider trusting.

I want to talk about something that doesn't get enough attention. The way chronic stress changes your relationship with pain. When your nervous system has been in overdrive for years, your pain threshold shifts. Things that shouldn't hurt, hurt. Things that should hurt, don't register at all. Your body's volume knob is broken.

This is what the research on central sensitization shows. The spinal cord and brain start amplifying pain signals. Fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue, irritable bowel - these aren't imaginary. They're the body's honest response to a nervous system that never got to complete its stress cycle. The energy that was meant for fight or flight got trapped. And trapped energy has to go somewhere.

Sit with that for a moment. If you've been told your pain isn't real, if you've been dismissed by doctors, if you've started to doubt your own body - I want you to know something. Your body isn't lying. It's speaking the only language it has. The question isn't whether the pain is real. The question is what your body is trying to tell you through it.

Notice without judgment. This is key. Your nervous system is not wrong. It's doing its job. It's trying to protect you. Thank it. Acknowledge it. Say internally "I see you. I know you're trying to keep me safe. We're okay right now."

Then practice with small doses of kindness. Let someone hold the door without analyzing their motives. Let a cashier smile at you without assuming they want something. Let a friend say something nice and just sit with the discomfort for three seconds before you deflect or change the subject.

Three seconds. That's it. That's the whole practice. Just three seconds of tolerating the discomfort of receiving kindness. Over time, those three seconds can become four, then five, then a whole minute. Your nervous system learns, slowly, that kindness can arrive and leave again without harm. That it doesn't have to brace. That it can, maybe, begin to soften.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link), which offers exercises for exactly this kind of gentle, gradual shift.

You might also need to grieve. Grieve the kindness you didn't get. Grieve the years of bracing. Grieve the relationships that couldn't work because your body didn't know how to receive love. Grief is not a failure. It's the body's way of processing what was lost. And processing what was lost is part of making room for what could be.

This work is slow. It's not linear. Some days you'll take three steps forward and four steps back. Some days you'll feel your body relax into someone's kindness and then immediately panic and pull away. That's not failure. That's the process. That's your nervous system doing exactly what it should be doing, trying to find a new balance.

There is no finish line here. No point where you're "fixed" and kindness feels easy and natural all the time. But there is a point where the flinch gets smaller. Where the bracing gets softer. Where you can feel warmth and not immediately scan for the exit. Where you can receive and not feel like you owe something in return.

And that, honestly, is enough. That's the goal. Not perfection. Just enough softening to let some light in.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering
  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - A book that connects the dots between stress and illness
